rawText:
When I was a kid, the disaster we worried about most was a nuclear war. That's why we had a barrel like this down in our basement, filled with cans of food and water. When the nuclear attack came, we were supposed to go downstairs, hunker down, and eat out of that barrel.

Today the greatest risk of global catastrophe doesn't look like this. Instead, it looks like this. If anything kills over 10 million people in the next few decades, it's most likely to be a highly infectious virus rather than a war. Not missiles, but microbes. Now, part of the reason for this is that we've invested a huge amount in nuclear deterrents. But we've actually invested very little in a system to stop an epidemic. We're not ready for the next epidemic.

Let's look at Ebola. I'm sure all of you read about it in the newspaper, lots of tough challenges. I followed it carefully through the case analysis tools we use to track polio eradication. And as you look at what went on, the problem wasn't that there was a system that didn't work well enough, the problem was that we didn't have a system at all. In fact, there's some pretty obvious key missing pieces.

We didn't have a group of epidemiologists ready to go, who would have gone, seen what the disease was, seen how far it had spread. The case reports came in on paper. It was very delayed before they were put online and they were extremely inaccurate.

The failure to prepare could allow the next epidemic to be dramatically more devastating than Ebola. Let's look at the progression of Ebola over this year. About 10,000 people died, and nearly all were in the three West African countries.

So next time, we might not be so lucky. You can have a virus where people feel well enough while they're infectious that they get on a plane or they go to a market. The source of the virus could be a natural epidemic like Ebola, or it could be bioterrorism. So there are things that would literally make things a thousand times worse.

In fact, let's look at a model of a virus spread through the air, like the Spanish Flu back in 1918. So here's what would happen: It would spread throughout the world very, very quickly. And you can see over 30 million people died from that epidemic. So this is a serious problem. We should be concerned.

But in fact, we can build a really good response system. We have the benefits of all the science and technology that we talk about here. We've got cell phones to get information from the public and get information out to them. We have satellite maps where we can see where people are and where they're moving. We have advances in biology that should dramatically change the turnaround time to look at a pathogen and be able to make drugs and vaccines that fit for that pathogen. So we can have tools, but those tools need to be put into an overall global health system. And we need preparedness.

overview.title: The Next Outbreak? We're Not Ready

sentences.original: When I was a kid, the disaster we worried about most was a nuclear war.
sentences.translation: 제가 어렸을 때, 우리가 가장 걱정했던 재난은 핵전쟁이었습니다.

grammarElements.text: the disaster we worried about most
grammarElements.label: 주어 (관계대명사 목적격 생략)

vocabulary.example: The earthquake was a terrible disaster.
Nuclear energy is a controversial topic.
Parents often worry about their children.

expressions.usage: 어린 시절 경험을 회상하며 이야기를 시작할 때
최상급의 의미를 부사 most로 표현

patterns.pattern: When I was [age/period], [main clause]
patterns.explanation: 과거 경험을 회상하며 이야기를 여는 전형적 패턴. TED 강연의 흔한 오프닝.
examples: When I was in college, I discovered my passion for science.
When I was growing up, technology was very different.

drillQuestions.question: _____ I was a kid, we worried about nuclear war.
The disaster we worried about _____ was a nuclear war.

sentences.notes: "the disaster we worried about most"에서 관계대명사(that/which)가 생략되어 있다. 원래는 "the disaster that we worried about most". 이는 구어체에서 매우 자연스러운 생략이다.

koreanAlignment.en: the disaster we worried about most

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 개인적 일화(personal anecdote) — 어린 시절 이야기로 청중의 공감을 유도하며 시작

sentences.original: That's why we had a barrel like this down in our basement, filled with cans of food and water.
sentences.translation: 그래서 우리 집 지하실에 이런 통이 있었습니다. 통조림과 물이 가득 채워져 있었죠.

grammarElements.text: filled with cans of food and water
grammarElements.label: 과거분사구 (barrel을 수식)

vocabulary.example: They stored water in a large barrel.
We keep old furniture in the basement.

expressions.usage: 원인과 결과를 연결할 때 사용
용기나 공간이 무언가로 채워져 있을 때

patterns.pattern: That's why [subject] [verb] ...
patterns.explanation: 앞선 내용의 이유를 설명하는 연결 패턴. 원인 → 결과 관계를 명확하게 표현.
examples: That's why I decided to study abroad.
That's why we need to prepare for emergencies.

drillQuestions.question: _____ why we had a barrel in our basement.
The barrel was _____ with cans of food and water.

sentences.notes: "filled with"는 과거분사의 형용사적 용법으로, barrel을 뒤에서 수식한다. "a barrel filled with ~"는 "~으로 가득 찬 통"이라는 의미.

koreanAlignment.en: we had a barrel like this
filled with cans of food and water

sentences.original: When the nuclear attack came, we were supposed to go downstairs, hunker down, and eat out of that barrel.
sentences.translation: 핵 공격이 오면, 아래층으로 내려가서 웅크리고 그 통에서 꺼내 먹어야 했습니다.

grammarElements.text: go downstairs, hunker down, and eat out of that barrel
grammarElements.label: 보어 (3개의 동사원형 병렬)

vocabulary.example: We hunkered down during the storm.
You're supposed to arrive by 9 AM.
They ate out of tin cans during the camping trip.

expressions.usage: 위험한 상황에서 안전한 곳에 머무를 때. 비유적으로 어려운 시기를 버틸 때도 사용

patterns.pattern: We were supposed to [verb A], [verb B], and [verb C]
patterns.explanation: 계획이나 규칙을 설명하는 패턴. be supposed to + 병렬 동사 구조.
examples: We were supposed to wake up, pack our bags, and leave by noon.
Students were supposed to read, discuss, and write a summary.

drillQuestions.question: We were supposed _____ go downstairs.
We had to hunker _____ and wait for safety.

sentences.notes: hunker down은 미국 영어에서 자주 쓰이는 표현으로, 물리적(웅크리다)과 비유적(견디다) 두 가지 의미가 있다. 세 동작(go, hunker, eat)의 병렬 구조가 당시 대비 계획의 단순함을 보여준다.

koreanAlignment.en: and eat out of that barrel

sentences.original: Today the greatest risk of global catastrophe doesn't look like this. Instead, it looks like this.
sentences.translation: 오늘날 전 세계적 대재앙의 가장 큰 위험은 이런 모습이 아닙니다. 대신, 이런 모습입니다.

grammarElements.text: the greatest risk of global catastrophe
grammarElements.label: 주어

vocabulary.example: Climate change could lead to a global catastrophe.
There is a risk of flooding after heavy rain.

expressions.phrase: doesn't look like this ... it looks like this
expressions.usage: 시각 자료와 함께 기대를 뒤엎는 대비를 만들 때
앞 내용과 반대되는 사실을 소개할 때

patterns.pattern: [Subject] doesn't look like [X]. Instead, it looks like [Y].
patterns.explanation: 기대와 현실의 차이를 극적으로 대비하는 패턴. 프레젠테이션에서 시각 자료 전환에 자주 사용.
examples: The future doesn't look like flying cars. Instead, it looks like AI.
Success doesn't look like perfection. Instead, it looks like persistence.

drillQuestions.question: The greatest risk _____ look like a nuclear war.
_____, it looks like a virus.

sentences.notes: 이 두 문장은 강연의 핵심 전환점. "this"는 각각 핵폭발 이미지와 바이러스 이미지를 가리키는 직시어(deixis). TED 강연 특유의 시각 자료 활용법이다.

koreanAlignment.en: the greatest risk of global catastrophe
koreanAlignment.ko: 전 세계적 대재앙의 가장 큰 위험은

pronunciationNotes: catastrophe: /kəˈtæstrəfi/ — "커태스트러피", 강세는 두 번째 음절. e는 묵음이 아님
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 대조(contrast) — 핵전쟁 이미지와 바이러스 이미지를 나란히 배치하여 논지를 극대화

sentences.original: If anything kills over 10 million people in the next few decades, it's most likely to be a highly infectious virus rather than a war.
sentences.translation: 만약 향후 수십 년 내에 1,000만 명 이상을 죽이는 것이 있다면, 그것은 전쟁보다는 전염성이 매우 높은 바이러스일 가능성이 가장 높습니다.

grammarElements.text: If anything kills over 10 million people in the next few decades
grammarElements.label: 조건절 (if)

vocabulary.example: The flu is highly infectious.
Technology has changed dramatically over the past decade.
It's likely to rain tomorrow.

expressions.usage: 가장 확률 높은 시나리오를 제시할 때
두 가지를 비교하여 하나를 선호하거나 더 가능성 있다고 할 때

patterns.pattern: If [something happens], it's most likely to be [X] rather than [Y]
patterns.explanation: 가정을 세운 뒤, X가 Y보다 더 가능성이 높음을 주장하는 패턴.
examples: If a crisis occurs, it's most likely to be economic rather than military.
If students fail, it's most likely to be due to lack of practice rather than ability.

drillQuestions.question: It's most likely to be a virus _____ than a war.
If anything kills millions, it's most _____ to be a virus.

sentences.notes: 이 문장은 강연의 핵심 주장. "if anything"은 "만약 무언가가 있다면"이라는 가정을 세우고, rather than으로 대중의 예상(전쟁)과 실제 위험(바이러스)을 대조한다. 2015년의 이 예측은 COVID-19로 현실화되었다.

koreanAlignment.en: If anything kills over 10 million people
koreanAlignment.ko: 만약 1,000만 명 이상을 죽이는 것이 있다면

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 예언적 경고(prophetic warning) — 미래의 위험을 구체적 수치(1,000만)와 함께 경고

sentences.original: Not missiles, but microbes.
sentences.translation: 미사일이 아니라, 미생물입니다.

vocabulary.example: The country tested a new missile.
Microbes are too small to see with the naked eye.

expressions.usage: 대비를 통해 핵심을 강조하는 수사법

patterns.pattern: Not [A], but [B].
patterns.explanation: 두 단어 또는 개념을 극적으로 대조하는 짧은 문장 패턴. 연설에서 인상적인 한 줄로 사용.
examples: Not luck, but hard work.
Not words, but actions.

drillQuestions.question: Not missiles, _____ microbes.

sentences.notes: 세 단어로 이루어진 극도로 압축된 문장. missiles과 microbes의 두운(alliteration, m-m)이 기억에 남는 효과를 만든다. 강연의 가장 인용이 많은 구절 중 하나.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 두운법(alliteration) + 대구법(antithesis) — m으로 시작하는 두 단어의 대비가 강렬한 인상

sentences.original: Now, part of the reason for this is that we've invested a huge amount in nuclear deterrents. But we've actually invested very little in a system to stop an epidemic.
sentences.translation: 이것의 원인 중 하나는 우리가 핵 억제력에 막대한 금액을 투자했기 때문입니다. 하지만 전염병을 막기 위한 시스템에는 실제로 거의 투자하지 않았습니다.

grammarElements.text: part of the reason for this
grammarElements.label: 주어

grammarElements.text: that we've invested a huge amount in nuclear deterrents
grammarElements.label: that절 (보어)

grammarElements.text: in a system to stop an epidemic
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (대상)

vocabulary.example: The company invested heavily in research.
Nuclear weapons serve as a deterrent against attack.
The flu epidemic affected millions of people.

expressions.phrase: part of the reason for this is that
expressions.usage: 복합적인 원인 중 하나를 설명할 때
투자 부족을 지적할 때

patterns.pattern: Part of the reason for this is that ... But we've actually [verb] very little in ...
patterns.explanation: 원인을 분석한 뒤 대조적인 현실(투자 부족)을 지적하는 논증 패턴.
examples: Part of the reason is that we focused on short-term gains. But we actually invested very little in long-term planning.

drillQuestions.question: Part of the reason _____ this is that we invested heavily.
We've invested very little _____ a system to stop an epidemic.

sentences.notes: a huge amount vs very little의 대비가 투자 불균형을 극적으로 보여준다. deterrent는 "억지하다"라는 뜻의 deter에서 파생된 명사.

koreanAlignment.en: part of the reason for this is
that we've invested a huge amount
But we've actually invested very little
in a system to stop an epidemic

sentences.original: We're not ready for the next epidemic.
sentences.translation: 우리는 다음 전염병에 대비되어 있지 않습니다.

vocabulary.example: Are you ready for the exam?

expressions.usage: 경고의 메시지를 직접적으로 전달할 때

patterns.pattern: We're not ready for [something]
patterns.explanation: 준비 부족을 경고하는 직접적이고 단순한 패턴. 강연 제목과 동일한 핵심 메시지.
examples: We're not ready for climate change.
We're not ready for the next financial crisis.

drillQuestions.question: We're not ready _____ the next epidemic.

sentences.notes: 강연 제목이기도 한 이 문장은 의도적으로 짧고 단순하게 구성되어 있다. 복잡한 설명 뒤에 오는 짧은 결론으로 임팩트를 극대화.
pronunciationNotes: epidemic: /ˌepɪˈdemɪk/ — 강세는 세 번째 음절 dem에 있음
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 반복(repetition) — 강연 제목을 본문에서 반복하여 핵심 메시지를 각인

sentences.original: And as you look at what went on, the problem wasn't that there was a system that didn't work well enough, the problem was that we didn't have a system at all.
sentences.translation: 무슨 일이 일어났는지 살펴보면, 문제는 잘 작동하지 않는 시스템이 있었던 것이 아닙니다. 문제는 시스템 자체가 아예 없었다는 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: as you look at what went on
grammarElements.label: 종속절 (시간 — as)

grammarElements.text: the problem wasn't that there was a system that didn't work well enough
grammarElements.label: 주절 (부정)

grammarElements.text: the problem was that we didn't have a system at all
grammarElements.label: 주절 (긍정 — 실제 문제)

vocabulary.example: What went on at the meeting?
I don't understand this at all.

expressions.phrase: the problem wasn't X, the problem was Y
expressions.usage: 문제의 본질을 재정의할 때 사용하는 강력한 대비 표현
완전한 부정을 강조할 때

patterns.pattern: The problem wasn't [X], the problem was [Y]
patterns.explanation: 문제를 재정의하는 대비 패턴. 청중의 가정을 뒤엎고 진짜 문제를 제시.
examples: The problem wasn't lack of talent, the problem was lack of opportunity.
The problem wasn't the technology, the problem was the process.

drillQuestions.question: The problem _____ that the system didn't work well enough.
We didn't have a system at ___.

sentences.notes: that이 3번 등장: (1) the problem was that (접속사), (2) there was a system that (관계대명사), (3) that we didn't have (접속사). 각각의 역할을 구분하는 것이 핵심.

koreanAlignment.en: as you look at what went on
there was a system that didn't work well enough
koreanAlignment.ko: 잘 작동하지 않는 시스템이 있었던 것

koreanAlignment.en: we didn't have a system at all

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 재정의(reframing) — 문제의 본질을 "시스템 부재"로 재정의하여 논점을 명확히

sentences.original: We didn't have a group of epidemiologists ready to go, who would have gone, seen what the disease was, seen how far it had spread.
sentences.translation: 즉시 출동할 준비가 된 역학조사관 그룹이 없었습니다. 그들이 있었다면 가서 질병이 무엇인지, 얼마나 퍼졌는지 확인했을 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: a group of epidemiologists ready to go
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (형용사구 후치수식)

grammarElements.text: who would have gone, seen what the disease was, seen how far it had spread
grammarElements.label: 관계대명사절 (가정법 과거완료)

vocabulary.example: Epidemiologists tracked the spread of the virus.
The fire spread quickly through the building.

expressions.usage: 즉시 행동에 나설 수 있는 상태를 표현
실제로는 일어나지 않은 가정의 상황을 표현

patterns.pattern: We didn't have [X] who would have [past participle] ...
patterns.explanation: 자원 부재를 지적하고, 가정법으로 "있었다면 했을 일"을 설명하는 패턴.
examples: We didn't have doctors who would have treated the patients.
We didn't have engineers who would have fixed the problem.

drillQuestions.question: We didn't have epidemiologists who would _____ gone to help.
They would have seen how far the disease _____ spread.

sentences.notes: who 관계대명사절 안에 가정법 과거완료(would have gone/seen)가 사용되어 "있었다면 ~했을 텐데"라는 비현실적 가정을 나타낸다. epidemiologist는 발음이 어려운 고급 어휘.

koreanAlignment.en: seen how far it had spread

pronunciationNotes: epidemiologist: /ˌepɪˌdiːmiˈɑːlədʒɪst/ — 7음절, "에피디미올러지스트", 이 강연에서 가장 어려운 단어

sentences.original: The case reports came in on paper. It was very delayed before they were put online and they were extremely inaccurate.
sentences.translation: 사례 보고서는 종이로 들어왔습니다. 온라인에 올리기까지 매우 지연되었고, 극도로 부정확했습니다.

vocabulary.example: The doctor filed a case report for the patient.
The flight was delayed by two hours.
The weather forecast was inaccurate.

expressions.usage: 디지털이 아닌 아날로그 방식으로 정보가 전달될 때
정보를 디지털화하여 인터넷에 게시할 때

patterns.pattern: It was very [adjective] before [subject] [were/was] [past participle]
patterns.explanation: 어떤 일이 완료되기까지 오래 걸렸음을 나타내는 패턴.
examples: It was very long before the results were announced.
It was very difficult before the system was fixed.

drillQuestions.question: The case reports came _____ on paper.
They were extremely _____.

sentences.notes: in- 접두사의 부정: inaccurate(부정확한) = in + accurate(정확한). on paper는 "종이 위에"라는 물리적 의미와 동시에 "구식 방법으로"라는 비유적 의미도 담고 있다.

koreanAlignment.en: The case reports came in on paper

sentences.original: The failure to prepare could allow the next epidemic to be dramatically more devastating than Ebola.
sentences.translation: 대비의 실패는 다음 전염병이 에볼라보다 훨씬 더 파괴적이 되도록 허용할 수 있습니다.

grammarElements.text: to be dramatically more devastating than Ebola
grammarElements.label: 목적격 보어 (to부정사 + 비교급)

vocabulary.example: The failure to act quickly caused serious problems.
The earthquake had devastating effects on the region.
The situation has changed dramatically.

expressions.usage: 준비하지 못한 것을 명사화하여 원인으로 제시
비교급을 부사로 강조하여 극적인 차이를 표현

patterns.pattern: The failure to [verb] could allow [object] to be [comparative]
patterns.explanation: 대비 부족이 초래할 수 있는 결과를 경고하는 패턴. allow + 목적어 + to be 구조.
examples: The failure to invest could allow competitors to be more successful.
The failure to study could allow your grades to be worse.

drillQuestions.question: The failure _____ prepare could be dangerous.
It could be dramatically more _____ than Ebola.

sentences.notes: allow + 목적어 + to부정사는 5형식 구조. "failure to prepare"는 명사 + to부정사 구조로, 동사를 명사화하여 문장의 주어로 사용. dramatically가 비교급(more devastating)을 강화하는 부사 역할.

sentences.original: So next time, we might not be so lucky. You can have a virus where people feel well enough while they're infectious that they get on a plane or they go to a market.
sentences.translation: 다음번에는 그렇게 운이 좋지 않을 수 있습니다. 사람들이 전염성이 있는 동안에도 충분히 건강하게 느껴서 비행기를 타거나 시장에 가는 바이러스가 있을 수 있습니다.

grammarElements.text: where people feel well enough while they're infectious
grammarElements.label: 관계부사절 (where)

grammarElements.text: that they get on a plane or they go to a market
grammarElements.label: 결과절 (so/enough...that)

vocabulary.example: We were lucky that no one was hurt.
The patient is still infectious.

expressions.meaning: 그렇게 운이 좋지 않을 수 있다
expressions.usage: 다음에는 상황이 달라질 수 있다는 경고
enough + that 결과절 구조로 정도와 결과를 연결

patterns.pattern: [Subject] feel [adjective] enough while ... that they [verb]
patterns.explanation: enough...that 구조. 충분한 정도를 표현하고, that 이하에서 그 결과를 설명.
examples: Students felt confident enough that they answered without hesitation.
He felt strong enough while recovering that he started walking.

drillQuestions.question: We _____ not be so lucky next time.
People feel well enough _____ they're infectious that they travel.

sentences.notes: 이 문장에는 where 관계부사절 안에 while 종속절이 내포되고, 다시 that 결과절로 이어지는 복합 구조. "feel well enough ... that"은 enough...that 구문으로 정도와 결과를 연결.

koreanAlignment.en: next time, we might not be so lucky
koreanAlignment.ko: 다음번에는 그렇게 운이 좋지 않을 수 있습니다

koreanAlignment.en: that they get on a plane
or they go to a market

sentences.original: The source of the virus could be a natural epidemic like Ebola, or it could be bioterrorism. So there are things that would literally make things a thousand times worse.
sentences.translation: 바이러스의 출처는 에볼라 같은 자연 전염병이거나, 생물학적 테러일 수 있습니다. 문자 그대로 상황을 천 배나 악화시킬 수 있는 것들이 있습니다.

grammarElements.text: that would literally make things a thousand times worse
grammarElements.label: 관계대명사절 (that — things를 수식)

vocabulary.example: Governments invest in defense against bioterrorism.
The explosion literally shook the building.
What is the source of this information?

expressions.usage: 배수 표현으로 극단적인 차이를 과장하여 강조

patterns.pattern: The source of [X] could be [A], or it could be [B]
patterns.explanation: 가능한 원인을 두 가지 이상 제시하는 패턴. could be로 가능성을 나타냄.
examples: The source of the problem could be hardware, or it could be software.
The cause could be stress, or it could be a medical condition.

drillQuestions.question: The source of the virus _____ be bioterrorism.
It would _____ make things a thousand times worse.

sentences.notes: bioterrorism은 bio(생물) + terrorism(테러)의 합성어. "a thousand times worse"는 배수 표현으로, 수치를 사용한 극적 강조법이다.

koreanAlignment.en: The source of the virus could be
there are things that would literally
make things a thousand times worse

sentences.original: In fact, let's look at a model of a virus spread through the air, like the Spanish Flu back in 1918.
sentences.translation: 사실, 1918년 스페인 독감처럼 공기를 통해 퍼지는 바이러스 모델을 살펴봅시다.

grammarElements.text: a model of a virus spread through the air
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (과거분사 spread가 virus를 수식)

grammarElements.text: like the Spanish Flu back in 1918
grammarElements.label: 비교/예시 (like)

vocabulary.example: Scientists built a model to predict weather patterns.
The virus spread through the air is especially dangerous.

expressions.usage: 청중의 주의를 새로운 자료로 유도할 때
back을 넣어 "과거에"라는 시간적 거리감을 강조

patterns.pattern: Let's look at [a model/example] of [X], like [Y]
patterns.explanation: 새로운 시각 자료나 예시를 소개하는 프레젠테이션 패턴.
examples: Let's look at a case of economic recovery, like South Korea in the 1990s.
Let's look at an example of innovation, like the smartphone.

drillQuestions.question: Let's look _____ a model of a virus.

sentences.notes: spread는 현재분사가 아닌 과거분사로, "공기를 통해 퍼진"이라는 수동적 의미. spread-spread-spread로 불규칙 변화한다. 스페인 독감은 1918-1919년에 5,000만~1억 명의 사망자를 낸 역사상 최악의 팬데믹.

koreanAlignment.en: a model of a virus spread through the air

sentences.original: So this is a serious problem. We should be concerned.
sentences.translation: 이것은 심각한 문제입니다. 우리는 우려해야 합니다.

vocabulary.example: Parents are concerned about online safety.

expressions.usage: 청중에게 심각성을 인식하도록 촉구할 때

patterns.pattern: This is a serious problem. We should be [adjective].
patterns.explanation: 문제 제기 후 행동/태도를 촉구하는 두 문장 연속 패턴.
examples: This is a growing crisis. We should be prepared.
This is a real threat. We should be vigilant.

drillQuestions.question: We _____ be concerned about this problem.

sentences.notes: 의도적으로 짧고 단순한 두 문장. 앞의 긴 설명 후에 오는 간결한 결론으로 메시지의 임팩트를 높인다.

sentences.original: But in fact, we can build a really good response system.
sentences.translation: 하지만 사실, 우리는 정말 좋은 대응 시스템을 구축할 수 있습니다.

vocabulary.example: The emergency response system worked efficiently.
We need to build a stronger healthcare system.

expressions.usage: 부정적인 내용 뒤에 긍정적 전환을 할 때

patterns.pattern: But in fact, we can [verb] ...
patterns.explanation: 이전의 문제 제기에서 해결책으로 전환하는 패턴. 희망적 메시지를 전달.
examples: But in fact, we can solve this problem with technology.
But in fact, we can prevent most of these issues.

drillQuestions.question: But in fact, we _____ build a good response system.

sentences.notes: 강연의 구조적 전환점. "문제"에서 "해결책"으로 넘어가는 문장. TED 강연의 전형적인 3부 구조(문제 제기 → 증거 → 해결책) 중 해결책 파트의 시작.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 전환(volta/turn) — 문제에서 해결책으로의 극적 전환, 청중에게 희망을 제시

sentences.original: We have the benefits of all the science and technology that we talk about here. We've got cell phones to get information from the public and get information out to them.
sentences.translation: 우리에게는 여기서 이야기하는 모든 과학과 기술의 혜택이 있습니다. 대중으로부터 정보를 얻고 그들에게 정보를 전달할 수 있는 휴대전화가 있습니다.

grammarElements.text: the benefits of all the science and technology
grammarElements.label: 목적어

grammarElements.text: to get information from the public and get information out to them
grammarElements.label: 목적 부사구 (to부정사)

vocabulary.example: Exercise has many health benefits.
The information is available to the public.

expressions.usage: have의 구어적 표현. TED 강연에서 자연스러운 톤을 유지하기 위해 사용
정보를 배포하거나 전파할 때

patterns.pattern: We have [tools] to [verb A] and [verb B]
patterns.explanation: 사용 가능한 도구와 그 용도를 나열하는 패턴.
examples: We have software to analyze data and generate reports.
We have the resources to train workers and build facilities.

drillQuestions.question: We have the _____ of all the science and technology.
We've got cell phones to get information _____ the public.

sentences.notes: "get information from"(정보를 얻다)과 "get information out to"(정보를 전달하다)의 방향 대비에 주목. 같은 동사 get이 전치사에 따라 반대 방향의 정보 흐름을 나타낸다.

koreanAlignment.en: to get information from the public
and get information out to them

sentences.original: We have advances in biology that should dramatically change the turnaround time to look at a pathogen and be able to make drugs and vaccines that fit for that pathogen.
sentences.translation: 생물학의 발전이 있어서, 병원체를 분석하고 그 병원체에 맞는 약과 백신을 만드는 소요 시간을 극적으로 단축할 수 있어야 합니다.

grammarElements.text: that should dramatically change the turnaround time
grammarElements.label: 관계대명사절 (that — advances를 수식)

grammarElements.text: and be able to make drugs and vaccines
grammarElements.label: 등위 부사구 (and + to부정사)

vocabulary.example: Recent advances in AI have been remarkable.
The turnaround time for test results is 48 hours.
The scientists identified the pathogen that caused the outbreak.
The vaccine was developed in record time.

expressions.usage: 어떤 과정이 시작에서 완료까지 걸리는 시간
특정 목적이나 대상에 맞는 것을 표현

patterns.pattern: We have advances in [field] that should [verb] ...
patterns.explanation: 특정 분야의 발전이 가져올 긍정적 변화를 설명하는 패턴.
examples: We have advances in medicine that should cure many diseases.
We have advances in computing that should speed up research.

drillQuestions.question: Advances in biology should dramatically change the _____ time.
We need drugs and vaccines that _____ for that pathogen.

sentences.notes: 관계대명사 that이 두 번 사용: 첫 번째는 advances를, 두 번째는 drugs and vaccines를 수식. pathogen은 의학/생물학 전문 용어로, COVID-19 이후 일상 영어에서도 자주 쓰이게 되었다.

koreanAlignment.en: and be able to make drugs and vaccines

pronunciationNotes: vaccine: /vækˈsiːn/ — "백신"이 아닌 "백씬", 강세는 두 번째 음절

sentences.original: So we can have tools, but those tools need to be put into an overall global health system. And we need preparedness.
sentences.translation: 도구는 가질 수 있지만, 그 도구들은 전반적인 글로벌 보건 시스템에 포함되어야 합니다. 그리고 우리에게는 대비 태세가 필요합니다.

vocabulary.example: The overall performance of the team was excellent.
Emergency preparedness saves lives.

expressions.usage: 도구나 자원이 더 큰 체계 안에 통합되어야 할 때

patterns.pattern: We can have [X], but [X] needs to be [past participle] into [system]
patterns.explanation: 도구의 존재만으로는 부족하고 시스템 통합이 필요함을 주장하는 패턴.
examples: We can have the data, but it needs to be integrated into the platform.
We can have talented people, but they need to be organized into effective teams.

drillQuestions.question: Those tools need to be _____ into a global health system.
We need _____ to face the next epidemic.

sentences.notes: preparedness는 prepared(준비된) + -ness(명사 접미사)로 만들어진 추상명사. 강연의 핵심 키워드로, 단순히 도구가 있는 것이 아니라 체계적 대비가 필요하다는 메시지.

koreanAlignment.en: but those tools need to be put into
